title: Spain
subtitle: 1975–today · Transition, Democracy and the Unfinished Nation

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Constitutional monarchy — from La Transición to today

entries.label: Juan Carlos I — architect of the Transition
entries.hint: Franco designates Juan Carlos as his successor expecting continuity — within two years Juan Carlos dismantles the dictatorship through legal reform from within; his decisive televised speech on 23 February 1981 stops the coup attempt and cements democratic legitimacy.
entries.note: Juan Carlos I's accession speech 22 Nov 1975: BOE 23 Nov 1975. His 23-F speech transcript: RTVE archive (broadcast live, 1:14am, 24 Feb 1981). The 153 declassified 23-F documents (Feb 2026): La Moncloa website — include telephone transcripts. Powell (1996), Juan Carlos of Spain: the standard English biography. He abdicated 18 Jun 2014: BOE 19 Jun 2014.

entries.label: Felipe VI — constitutional king
entries.hint: Felipe VI accedes on his father's abdication — his reign is defined by the 2017 Catalan independence crisis, during which his televised address (3 October 2017) sides explicitly with constitutional order against the referendum; a significant departure from his father's consensual style.
entries.note: Abdication law of Juan Carlos I: BOE 19 Jun 2014. Felipe VI's 3 Oct 2017 speech: RTVE broadcast, transcript on La Moncloa. Constitutionally, the speech's political content was unusual — analysed by constitutional scholars in real time. The reign's constitutional status: documented in BOE throughout.

entries.label: Adolfo Suárez — first democratic prime minister
entries.hint: Suárez, a former Franco official, is the paradox of the Transition: a man of the regime who dismantles it from within; his Ley para la Reforma Política (1977) transforms the Francoist Cortes into a democratic parliament through a self-abolishing vote.
entries.note: Suárez's appointment by Juan Carlos: BOE 5 Jul 1976. Ley para la Reforma Política, ratified by Francoist Cortes 18 Nov 1976: BOE 5 Jan 1977. Referendum approving the LRP, 15 Dec 1976: Interior Ministry results. Powell (1996). The Cortes voting itself out of existence: documented in Diario de Sesiones. Suárez resigned Jan 1981, weeks before the coup attempt.

entries.label: Felipe González — Socialist transformation
entries.hint: González wins an absolute majority in 1982 — the Socialists govern for 14 years, overseeing EU accession, NATO integration, economic modernisation and the welfare state's expansion; his government ends in corruption scandals (GAL affair, Filesa case).
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 3 Dec 1982. Re-elections: 1986, 1989, 1993 (minority). EU accession 1986: under his government. GAL affair (state-sponsored anti-ETA death squads): documented in Audiencia Nacional records and Diario 16 investigative reporting 1988–1995. Resignation 1996: BOE 6 May 1996. Gillespie (1989), The Spanish Socialist Party: the standard academic study.

entries.label: José María Aznar — conservative modernisation
entries.hint: Aznar wins in 1996 on economic reform and anti-corruption — oversees Spain's entry into the euro (1999), an economic boom, and controversial support for the Iraq War (2003); the mishandling of the 11-M bombings (blaming ETA) costs his party the election three days later.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 6 May 1996. Spain's euro entry 1 Jan 1999: BOE. Iraq War support: Aznar's signature on the 'Letter of Eight' (Wall Street Journal, 30 Jan 2003) — the primary document. The 20M anti-war protest 15 Feb 2003: Interior Ministry estimates. 11-M and electoral defeat: Interior Ministry election results 14 Mar 2004.

entries.label: José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero — social liberalism
entries.hint: Zapatero withdraws troops from Iraq within days of taking office, legalises same-sex marriage (2005), passes the Historical Memory Law (2007) and gender violence law — then presides over the catastrophic 2008 crash and austerity; he declines to seek a third term.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 18 Apr 2004. Iraq withdrawal order: BOE Apr 2004. Same-sex marriage: Ley 13/2005, BOE 2 Jul 2005. Historical Memory Law: BOE 27 Dec 2007. The 2010 austerity decree: BOE 12 May 2010. Resignation: announced Jan 2011, new elections Nov 2011. Público and El País archives document the government's record in real time.

entries.label: Mariano Rajoy — austerity and the Catalan crisis
entries.hint: Rajoy governs through the deepest austerity in Spanish democratic history — unemployment peaks at 27%; his government falls in a no-confidence motion over corruption (Gürtel case), the first such dismissal in Spanish democratic history.
entries.note: Appointment: BOE 22 Dec 2011. Austerity measures: multiple BOE decrees 2012–2013. Gürtel corruption case: Audiencia Nacional records, sentence 2018. No-confidence motion (moción de censura) 1 Jun 2018: Diario de Sesiones del Congreso — the first successful no-confidence vote in Spanish democratic history. The Catalan referendum and Article 155 activation: BOE Oct 2017.

entries.label: Pedro Sánchez — minority government and fragmentation
entries.hint: Sánchez reaches power through the no-confidence vote against Rajoy — the first socialist PM without winning an election; governs through four elections in four years (2019×2, 2023), COVID-19, the Catalan amnesty law controversy, and the fragmentation of the party system.
entries.note: Appointment after no-confidence: BOE 2 Jun 2018. Re-elected Jan 2020 (coalition with Unidas Podemos — first coalition government in Spanish democratic history): BOE 8 Jan 2020. 2023 election results: Interior Ministry — PSOE won fewer seats than PP but formed government with Junts support and amnesty law. Amnesty Law: BOE May 2024. The 153 declassified 23-F documents (Feb 2026): announced by Sánchez as 'settling a historical debt' — La Moncloa press release.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: ETA terrorism, the 23-F coup attempt and the 11-M bombings

entries.label: Assassination of Admiral Carrero Blanco — 1973
entries.hint: ETA plants a bomb under Carrero Blanco's car — the explosion launches the car over a five-storey building; Carrero Blanco was Franco's designated successor and the assassination changes the course of the Transition by removing the hardest-line Francoist from succession.
entries.note: Police and judicial records: AHN. Contemporary press: El País archive, ABC archive. The engineering of the tunnel: documented in ETA's own later accounts. Preston (2012), Franco's Caudillo, ch. final. The assassination's political impact — removing the most rigid Francoist from succession — is argued by Preston (1993) and Payne (1987) as inadvertently facilitating the Transition.

entries.label: 23-F — attempted coup in parliament, 1981
entries.loc: Madrid (Congress of Deputies); Valencia (tanks in streets)
entries.hint: Lt Col Antonio Tejero storms the Congress of Deputies with 200 Civil Guards — the entire government is held hostage for 18 hours; Juan Carlos's televised address at 1:14am defeats the coup; the events are captured on television and radio.
entries.note: EXCEPTIONAL ANCHOR: RTVE footage (extant, broadcast live) — Tejero entering Congress captured on parliamentary cameras. King's speech broadcast 1:14am, 24 Feb 1981: RTVE archive. 153 declassified documents (25 Feb 2026): La Moncloa — include transcripts of telephone conversations between military commanders (Euronews, 25 Feb 2026). Tejero convicted 1982: Tribunal Supremo records.

entries.label: ETA's final ceasefire declaration — 2011
entries.hint: ETA declares a 'definitive cessation of armed activity' on 20 October 2011 — ending 43 years of terrorism that killed over 800 people; the declaration comes after a sustained international and judicial pressure campaign.
entries.note: ETA declaration 20 Oct 2011: published in full in Gara (Basque newspaper) and international press. The full text: archived in Centro Memorial de las Víctimas del Terrorismo (Vitoria). The death toll of 829: documented in Interior Ministry records. ETA's formal dissolution 3 May 2018: Interior Ministry announcement. The verification process: facilitated by international observers including Brian Currin (South Africa).

entries.label: 11-M Madrid train bombings — 2004
entries.loc: Madrid (Atocha station; Téllez street; Pozo station)
entries.hint: Al-Qaeda-linked bombers detonate ten improvised explosive devices on four commuter trains — 193 killed, 2,000 injured; the most deadly terrorist attack in Spanish history; the government's initial false attribution to ETA (three days before the general election) is considered a factor in its electoral defeat.
entries.note: Judicial investigation: Audiencia Nacional, Sumario 20/2004 — the trial records are public and extensive. Commission of Inquiry report (2005): Congreso de los Diputados archive. The misattribution to ETA: documented in real-time press records and government communications (AHN). The election results 14 Mar 2004: Interior Ministry archive. 192 victims (one died later): official memorial registry.

entries.label: 15-M Indignados movement — Puerta del Sol occupied
entries.hint: Hundreds of thousands occupy central squares across Spain on 15 May 2011 — protesting austerity, political corruption and the two-party system; the Puerta del Sol camp lasts weeks; the movement directly inspires Podemos (2014) and later Occupy Wall Street.
entries.note: The 15-M manifesto (¡Democracia Real YA!): published in full, archived at BNE digital. Turnout estimates: Interior Ministry (ranging 130,000–2,000,000+ — dispute itself is significant). The camp in Sol: documented in El País and El Mundo from 15 May 2011. Toret (2013), Tecnopolítica: the first academic study. The connection to Podemos founding: Iglesias (2015), Politics in a Time of Crisis — Podemos leader's own account. Castells (2012), Networks of Outrage and Hope: places 15-M in global comparative context.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Constitution of 1978 and the legal architecture of democracy

entries.label: Constitution of 1978 — the Magna Carta of the Transition
entries.hint: The 1978 Constitution is approved by referendum (88.5% in favour, 67.1% turnout) — establishing parliamentary monarchy, regional autonomy, and a rights framework; it is the product of consenso (political consensus) across parties including the Communist Party.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: BOE 29 Dec 1978 — the full constitutional text. Referendum result (6 Dec 1978): Interior Ministry official results. The constitutional drafting process (los padres de la Constitución): documented in the Cortes archives, Diario de Sesiones 1977–78. The Communist Party's role: Santiago Carrillo's PCE participated in drafting — its inclusion was itself a political act. Peces-Barba (2003), La elaboración de la Constitución de 1978.

entries.label: State of Autonomies — 17 regions self-governing
entries.hint: The 1978 Constitution creates the Estado de las Autonomías — 17 autonomous communities each with their own parliament and government; the Basque Country and Catalonia gain the most extensive powers; the structure is a deliberate ambiguity about whether Spain is federal or unitary.
entries.note: Basque Statute of Autonomy (Estatuto de Gernika): BOE 22 Dec 1979. Catalan Statute (Estatut de Sau): BOE 18 Dec 1979. The subsequent statutes for other regions: BOE throughout 1981–83. The constitutional ambiguity: Morán (2009) on the 'state of autonomies' as an unfinished settlement. The 17 autonomías: documented in Ministerio de las Administraciones Públicas records.

entries.label: Pactos de la Moncloa — the economic consensus
entries.hint: All major political parties sign the Moncloa Pacts — agreeing wage restraint, political reform, and economic stabilisation simultaneously; the pacts are the economic foundation of the Transition, preventing the inflation-triggered crisis that derailed other democratisations.
entries.note: Text of the Moncloa Pacts: BOE 27 Oct 1977. Signed by 7 parties including PCE (Communist) and AP (conservative): documented. Fuentes Quintana (1983), Economía y política en la Transición. The pacts as economic model: cited in international democratic transition literature (O'Donnell & Schmitter 1986).

entries.label: Historical Memory Law — exhumations and recognition
entries.hint: The 2007 Ley de Memoria Histórica mandates a mass graves programme, removes Francoist symbols from public space, and recognises victims of the Civil War and dictatorship — the 2022 Democratic Memory Law strengthens it; both laws are contested politically.
entries.note: Ley 52/2007 de Memoria Histórica: BOE 27 Dec 2007. Ley 20/2022 de Memoria Democrática: BOE 20 Oct 2022. Mass graves database: Ministerio de Presidencia, online. Centro Documental de la Memoria Histórica (Salamanca): the primary archive. The law's political contestation: documented in parliamentary debates (Diario de Sesiones). The PP government's suspension of funding 2011–2015: documented in budget records.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Secularisation, the Church's retreat and plural religious Spain

entries.label: Church-State separation in the 1978 Constitution
entries.hint: Article 16 of the 1978 Constitution declares Spain a non-confessional state while acknowledging the 'special relations' with the Catholic Church — a carefully negotiated formula that ends National Catholicism without confronting the Church directly.
entries.note: Constitution Article 16: BOE 29 Dec 1978. The 1979 Agreements between Spain and the Holy See: BOE 15 Dec 1979 — replace the 1953 Concordat with four partial agreements on legal status, education, military service and economic affairs. The secularisation data: INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadística) surveys document the rapid decline in Mass attendance from ~70% (1975) to ~15% (2020). This is a documented CATEGORICAL SHIFT in how the state defines itself.

entries.label: Muslim community and the Islamic Council of Spain
entries.hint: The 1992 Cooperation Agreement between the Spanish state and the Islamic Commission of Spain formally recognises Islam — reflecting the arrival of significant Muslim immigrant communities; Ceuta and Melilla on the African coast have majority-Muslim populations predating the agreements.
entries.note: Ley 26/1992 (Islamic Cooperation Agreement): BOE 12 Nov 1992. The CIE (Comisión Islámica de España): formally constituted 1992. INE data on Muslim population: from ~150,000 (1990) to ~2 million (2020). The pre-existing Muslim community in Ceuta and Melilla: documented in local civil registry records from the 19th century. Moreras (1999), Musulmanes en Barcelona: the first major sociological study.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: La Movida, cinema, architecture and the cultural explosion

entries.label: Pedro Almodóvar and La Movida Madrileña
entries.hint: La Movida — the creative explosion following Franco's death — produces Almodóvar's first films (Pepi, Luci, Bom, 1980; What Have I Done to Deserve This?, 1984), Andy Warhol's visits to Madrid, and a deliberate transgression of Francoist repression through sexual and artistic freedom.
entries.note: Almodóvar's Pepi, Luci, Bom (1980): first film, made for 500,000 pesetas — production records at El Deseo SA. All About My Mother (1999), Todo sobre mi madre: won the Oscar for Best Foreign Film — Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences records. The Movida's documentation: Ministerio de Cultura archives, press coverage in El País (first edition 4 May 1976 — itself a primary source of the Transition).

entries.label: Guggenheim Bilbao — the titanium catalyst
entries.hint: Frank Gehry's Guggenheim Museum Bilbao opens in 1997 — the titanium-clad building transforms a post-industrial waterfront city and triggers the 'Bilbao effect' concept in urban regeneration; the museum receives 1 million visitors in its first year.
entries.note: Extant (Museo Guggenheim Bilbao). Opening 18 Oct 1997: contemporary press records. Construction cost €84 million: Guggenheim Foundation records. The 'Bilbao effect': Plaza et al. (2003), JAPA 69(1) — the academic term coined here. The museum's visitor statistics: Guggenheim annual reports. Ibelings (1998), Supermodernism: the architectural context.

entries.label: Rosalía — flamenco fusion and global reach
entries.hint: Rosalía Vila Tobella's album El Mal Querer (2018) fuses flamenco with electronic production — winning the Latin Grammy for Best Fusion/Urban Interpretation and the Grammy for Best Latin/Urban Album; the most internationally successful Spanish musical artist since Julio Iglesias.
entries.note: El Mal Querer (2018): Sony Music. Latin Grammy winner 2018 (documented). Grammy Award winner 2023: Recording Academy records. The album's flamenco sources: based on a 13th-century Occitan novel Flamenca — the literary source documented in liner notes. Rosalía's conservatory training: Escola Superior de Música de Catalunya records. The album is currently the most studied recent work of Spanish popular music in academic literature.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Democracy, nationalism and the contested memory of the past

entries.label: Transition model — Spain as democratic template
entries.hint: Spain's Transition becomes the global reference model for democratic transitions — O'Donnell and Schmitter cite it in their foundational 1986 study; the consenso approach (negotiation rather than rupture) is exported as a model for Eastern Europe, Latin America and South Africa.
entries.note: O'Donnell & Schmitter (1986), Transitions from Authoritarian Rule: Tentative Conclusions — the foundational comparative politics text, cites Spain repeatedly. Gunther, Sani & Shabad (1986), Spain after Franco: the first systematic academic study. The model's influence on South Africa's transition: Asmal (1994) explicitly references the Spanish pactos. The Transition's limitations — the 'pact of silence' on the past — are the subject of the Historical Memory debates.

entries.label: El País — newspaper of the Transition
entries.hint: El País is founded on 4 May 1976 — the first major liberal newspaper of the Transition; its first edition is itself a primary source for the period; its archives document the entire democratic era in real time.
entries.note: First edition: 4 May 1976 — BNE and El País archive. Circulation: 450,000 daily at its peak (1990s). The paper's role in the Transition: Seoane & Sueiro (2004), Una historia de El País. The paper's archives: digitised from 1976 — the fullest newspaper record of democratic Spain. Note: El País is owned by the PRISA group — its editorial line (centrist-left) must be accounted for in using it as a primary source.

entries.label: Podemos and the end of bipartidismo — 2014–2015
entries.hint: Podemos is founded at the Complutense University on 16 January 2014 — within four months it wins 5 MEPs in the European elections; in December 2015 it enters the Congress of Deputies with 69 seats, ending the PP-PSOE duopoly that had governed Spain since 1982.
entries.note: Founding assembly 16 Jan 2014: documented in El País, Público (17 Jan 2014). European election results 25 May 2014: Interior Ministry — Podemos 7.98%, 5 MEPs. General election 20 Dec 2015: Interior Ministry — PP 123 seats, PSOE 90, Podemos 69, Ciudadanos 40 (first four-party parliament). Vox's first seats (Dec 2019): 52 deputies — Interior Ministry. This is a documented CATEGORICAL SHIFT: from bipartidismo to multi-party fragmentation in one electoral cycle.

categories.label: Subsistence
categories.subtitle: The Spanish economic miracle, boom and crash

entries.label: Spain joins the European Community — 1986
entries.hint: Spain's EU accession on 1 January 1986 ends nearly a decade of negotiations — it triggers massive structural funds, foreign investment and economic integration; Spain's GDP per capita rises from 70% of EU average (1985) to 90% (2007).
entries.note: Accession Treaty signed 12 Jun 1985: Archivo del Ministerio de Asuntos Exteriores (Madrid). Entered into force 1 Jan 1986: Official Journal of the European Communities, L302, 15 Nov 1985. GDP convergence data: Eurostat. The accession negotiations (1977–1985): documented in AMAE. Fuentes Quintana (1989) on the economic consequences.

entries.label: 2008 financial crisis — Spain's property collapse
entries.hint: Spain's property bubble — the largest in the world per capita at its peak — collapses in 2008; unemployment reaches 27% (2013); the EU-IMF bailout of €100bn (2012) is the largest bank rescue in Spanish history; 400,000 mortgage evictions documented 2008–2014.
entries.note: Eviction data: Consejo General del Poder Judicial (CGPJ) annual reports — 400,000+ between 2008 and 2014. Unemployment peak 27%: INE, Encuesta de Población Activa Q1 2013. EU bank rescue memorandum, Jul 2012: European Stability Mechanism records. Salmon (2010), The Modern Spanish Economy: the standard economic history, updated. The political consequences: rise of Podemos and Ciudadanos documented in electoral records from 2015.

entries.label: Housing affordability crisis — Spain's defining issue of the 2020s
entries.hint: Spain's housing crisis becomes the most politically dominant domestic issue by 2023–2025 — rental prices rising 50–100% in major cities since 2015; the 2023 Housing Law introduces rent caps; mass protests ('we can't afford to live here') draw hundreds of thousands in 2024.
entries.note: Rental price data: Idealista and INE housing statistics — documented annual increases. Housing Law (Ley de Vivienda): BOE 25 May 2023. The April 2024 housing protests: documented in El País, RTVE — 100,000+ in Barcelona alone. The Airbnb/tourist flat effect on rental markets: Idealista data, municipal reports from Barcelona and Madrid. The issue's political salience: CIS monthly barometer 2023–2024 shows housing as top concern for the first time. Noteworthy: this is ongoing — uncertainty must be flagged.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: European integration, immigration and global Spain

entries.label: NATO membership — 1982 accession and 1986 referendum
entries.hint: Spain joins NATO in 1982 under Calvo-Sotelo — then González holds a referendum on remaining (1986) having promised withdrawal; the 'Yes to NATO' campaign reverses González's own position; Spain stays in NATO (52.5% yes); the episode reveals the Socialists' European integration logic.
entries.note: NATO accession 30 May 1982: NATO records. Referendum 12 Mar 1986: Interior Ministry results (52.5% yes, 39.8% no, 7.7% blank). The campaign's reversal: documented in PSOE internal records and press. The three-condition 'yes': documented in González's statement (BOE). Spain's full military integration (joining the integrated command): 1999 — NATO records.

entries.label: Mass immigration to Spain — 1990s–2000s
entries.hint: Spain transforms from a country of emigrants to one of the world's fastest-growing immigration destinations — from 500,000 foreign residents (1995) to 5.6 million (2008); Moroccan, Romanian, Ecuadorian and other communities create new social realities documented in census and civil registry.
entries.note: INE Padrón Municipal (municipal register): the primary source — records all residents, citizens or not, by nationality. Data series 1996–2008: INE website. Izquierdo & Cornelius (2012), Controlling Immigration: Spain chapter — the standard academic analysis. The Delegación del Gobierno para la Inmigración y la Emigración annual reports: primary government documentation.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Democratic Spain in its European and global context

entries.label: Catalan independence crisis — 2017 referendum
entries.hint: The Catalan government holds an independence referendum declared illegal by the Constitutional Court — 2.3 million vote (90% yes, 43% turnout); Spanish police violently block polling stations; the Catalan president Puigdemont flees to Belgium; the crisis remains unresolved.
entries.note: Referendum results: Generalitat de Catalunya, 1 Oct 2017. Constitutional Court ruling (ATC 24/2017): declaring the referendum illegal. Puigdemont's exile in Belgium: Belgian court records. The UN Human Rights Committee ruling (2023): found Spain violated Puigdemont's right to stand for election. Felipe VI's 3 Oct 2017 speech: RTVE archive. The 2021 pardons of imprisoned leaders: BOE 22 Jun 2021.

entries.label: Spain's democratic consolidation as a European model
entries.hint: Spain becomes the model for democratic transitions — Chilean, Argentine and South African negotiators explicitly study the Spanish Transition; the consenso method and the Moncloa Pacts are referenced in South Africa's CODESA negotiations (1991–1993).
entries.note: O'Donnell & Schmitter (1986): the primary academic source establishing Spain as the model. Asmal et al. (1994), Reconciliation Through Truth — explicitly references the Spanish experience. The ANC's study of the Spanish Transition: documented in ANC internal papers (University of Fort Hare archive, South Africa). Huntington (1991), The Third Wave: places Spain at the centre of the global democratisation pattern.

entries.label: COVID-19 pandemic — Spain among the worst hit
entries.hint: Spain is among the first European countries overwhelmed by COVID-19 — Madrid's hospitals collapse in March 2020; Spain declares a state of alarm (14 March), one of Europe's strictest lockdowns; official deaths exceed 120,000 by mid-2021; the pandemic triggers a €140bn EU recovery fund allocation.
entries.note: State of alarm decree: Real Decreto 463/2020, BOE 14 Mar 2020. Official death toll: INE and Centro Nacional de Epidemiología data — disputed between official (87,000) and excess mortality estimates (120,000+). EU recovery fund (Plan de Recuperación, Transformación y Resiliencia): BOE and European Commission documentation. The nursing home deaths (residencias de mayores): Defensor del Pueblo inquiry report (2021). The pandemic's political impact: regional competence disputes documented in Constitutional Court records.

entries.label: 15-F anti-Iraq War protests — largest in Spanish history
entries.hint: Between 10 and 11 million Spaniards protest against the Iraq War on 15 February 2003 — making Spain the country with the highest per-capita anti-war turnout globally; Aznar's support for the war despite 90% public opposition becomes the defining political wound of his second term.
entries.note: Turnout estimates: Interior Ministry (650,000 in Madrid alone); organisers (2 million Madrid). National total: 10–11 million (organisers); widely cited as largest protest in Spanish history. Aznar's 'Letter of Eight' (WSJ, 30 Jan 2003): the primary document of his Iraq position. Public opinion: CIS survey Feb 2003 — 91% of Spaniards opposed the war (CIS study 2481). The protest's connection to the 11-M electoral result: argued in press, documented in CIS post-election surveys.

entries.label: Prestige oil spill — Nunca Máis
entries.hint: The tanker Prestige sinks off the Galician coast releasing 77,000 tonnes of oil — the worst environmental disaster in Spanish history; the government's delayed response ('the black thread moves away') provokes the Nunca Máis (Never Again) movement and hundreds of thousands of volunteers.
entries.note: The sinking: 13 Nov 2002 (split and sank 19 Nov). Official investigation: Ministerio de Fomento records. Environmental damage: documented in Xunta de Galicia and CSIC (Spanish National Research Council) studies. Volunteer numbers: estimated 300,000+ — documented in contemporary press and municipal records. The 'hilo negro' (black thread) quote by Environment Minister Clos: press archive 18 Nov 2002. Criminal proceedings: Tribunal Supremo ruling 2013. The Nunca Máis movement: its manifesto and records archived at Consello da Cultura Galega.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Same-sex marriage legalised — 2005
entries.hint: Spain legalises same-sex marriage on 30 June 2005 — the third country in the world after the Netherlands and Belgium; Prime Minister Zapatero explicitly frames it as completing the democratic revolution begun in 1978; the Constitutional Court upholds it in 2012.
entries.note: Ley 13/2005: BOE 2 Jul 2005 — the law text. The Congressional vote (30 Apr 2005): 183 in favour, 136 against — Diario de Sesiones del Congreso. Constitutional Court Ruling STC 198/2012 (6 Nov 2012): upholds the law 8–3. Spain was the third country globally and the first majority-Catholic country. The law's social context: CIS (Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas) surveys document public opinion shifts 1985–2005.

entries.label: 8-M feminist strikes — 2018 and 2019
entries.hint: Spain's 8 March feminist strikes (2018, 2019) are the largest women's mobilisations in Spanish history — 5.3 million march in 2018 according to organisers; the strikes produce Spain's most intense public debate on gender violence, equal pay and political representation.
entries.note: Strike turnout: Delegación del Gobierno para la Violencia de Género data; police counts lower (120,000 in Madrid alone). The Manifiesto 8-M (2018): published in full in major press. The legal debate around the strike's legality: Audiencia Nacional rulings. The subsequent 'purple tide' legislation — Ley Orgánica de Violencia de Género was already in place (LO 1/2004: BOE 29 Dec 2004) — but the 2018 strikes accelerated the Ley del Solo Sí es Sí (sexual consent law, 2022).

entries.label: Gender identity law — 2023
entries.hint: Spain passes the 'Trans Law' (Ley 4/2023 para la igualdad real y efectiva de las personas trans) allowing legal gender change by self-declaration without medical requirements — one of the most progressive gender identity laws in Europe; passed despite internal PSOE opposition.
entries.note: Ley 4/2023: BOE 2 Mar 2023. The congressional vote (188 in favour, 150 against, 12 abstentions): Diario de Sesiones, 16 Feb 2023. The internal PSOE debate: documented in party records and press. The contrasting debate between trans rights advocates and some feminist groups: documented in El País and Público archives 2020–2023. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: from medicalised gender identity to self-declaration.

entries.label: Romani community — gitanos in democratic Spain
entries.hint: Spain's 750,000 Romani (gitano) population is the largest in Western Europe — the Constitution forbids ethnic discrimination but structural inequalities are documented in education, housing and employment; the Fundación Secretariado Gitano produces annual reports measuring the gap between legal equality and lived reality.
entries.note: Fundación Secretariado Gitano, Discriminación y Comunidad Gitana: annual reports from 2004 (primary data source). INE population estimates: ~750,000–1 million. The gitano community's legal status under democracy: Constitutional Court jurisprudence on Roma rights. Laparra (2007), La exclusión social en España: the standard academic study. The community's cultural role in flamenco: documented in ethnomusicological literature.
